Severe and Lightning DE on 7/6.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused two discharge holes in the roof of a home on Autumnwood Way in Indian River Hundred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and severe thunderstorms developed along a cold frontal boundary during the afternoon of the 4th over northern Virginia and then moved across the Delmarva Peninsula during the evening of the 4th. In addition to the severe weather, the thunderstorms were prolific lightning producers. Between 8 p.m. EDT and 11 p.m. EDT about 4,700 cloud-to-surface lightning strikes occurred across the Delmarva Peninsula and adjacent coastal waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms across the southern tier of Sussex County tore down a few trees, tree limbs and wires from Delmar east to Millville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal passage triggered severe thunderstorms across the northern half of Delaware on the afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn spotter reported penny size hail in Bear. The same thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 55 mph in Delaware City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary helped trigger thunderstorms with torrential downpours across southern Delaware.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving and repeating thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage, field and flooding of smaller streams in and around Dagsboro Hundred. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 6 and 7 inches within Dagsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary helped trigger thunderstorms with torrential downpours across southern Delaware.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused roadway, stream and poor drainage flooding along the Milford and Mispillion border in southern Kent County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 3.1 inches within an hour in that area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enhanced rip currents and a pounding surf caused by swells from Hurricane Bertha caused many injuries and water rescues along the Delaware coast. The effects of Bertha started on the 11th, peaked on the 13th and 14th and slowly subsided over the next couple of days. About 55 medical emergencies were reported from surf scrapes to broken bones. Even one lifeguard was injured. In Rehoboth Beach, there were twenty-one broken ankles, fractured shoulders and dislocated knees reported. In addition, there were sixteen spinal injuries that required backboarding victims to emergency rooms. Water rescues started on the 11th in Fenwick Island as six children between the ages of 7 and 10 were caught in a rip current. Over the weekend of July 12th and 13th, about 35 bathers were rescued at Rehoboth Beach, 18 at Bethany Beach and 15 at Fenwick Island.

An approaching cold front that ended a week long heat wave also triggered severe thunderstorms across Delaware during the afternoon and early evening on the 23rd. The thunderstorms caused outages to about 9,000 homes and businesses in New Castle County. There were still 6,000 homes and businesses without power at 11 p.m. EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and wires in  northeastern New Castle County from Elsmere northeast into Brandywine Hundred. One downed tree blocked a roadway near U.S. Route 202 in Brandywine Hundred. Some pea size hail also occurred in the area. Power outages affected the Trolley Square section of Wilmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal passage triggered severe thunderstorms across the northern half of Delaware on the afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires in central Kent County in and around Dover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm caused torrential downpours to fall over  Blackbird Hundred in New Castle County. Poor drainage and small stream flooding occurred. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 2 and 3 inches, most of which fell within one hour.

Prior to the arrival of Tropical Storm Hanna, unseasonably dry weather continued in Delaware. Only two percent of the top soil in Delaware had adequate moisture. Eighty-five percent of the sampled soil conditions were described as very short on moisture. Stream flow was well below normal and Newark had to switch its water supply source from the White Clay Creek to its reservoir. The drop in freshwater runoff increased chloride and salt levels in tidal sections of the White Clay Creek and the Christina River. The United Water Company had to maintain an inflatable dam south of Stanton on the White Clay Creek to block salty tidal surges and trap freshwater supplies.||Starting with Tropical Storm Hanna, slightly above normal precipitation fell for the rest of the month. The rain came too late for the corn crop and early soybeans. Yields for corn were expected to be down as much as 50 percent on some farms. There was hope for the late soybean, wheat and barley crop. Farmers were hit with a double whammy as fuel and fertilizer costs rocketed. The state poultry industry was also feeling the pinch as food costs were rising because of the drought and shortages.  ||On a county weighted average September monthly precipitation ranged from 4.0 inches in Kent County to 4.7 inches in Sussex County and 5.0 inches in New Castle. Normal is around 3.7 inches.

Tropical Storm Hanna brought heavy rain and strong winds in Delaware and some minor tidal flooding in Delaware Bay on the 6th. Rain moved into the region during the morning, fell heavy at times in the afternoon and ended during the early evening. Storm totals ranged from around 1 to around 3.5 inches with the highest amounts in New Castle County. The strongest winds occurred during the late morning and afternoon with peak gusts as high as 53 mph. About 10,000 homes and businesses lost power on the Delmarva Peninsula. All power was restored by the 7th. Minor tidal flooding occurred in Delaware Bay during the afternoon as the surge averaged two to three feet. Many planned outdoor activities were cancelled.  ||The heavy rain caused minor roadway and low lying area flooding. The unseasonably dry weather leading into Hanna prevented stream and river flooding from occurring. The pounding surf caused about a three foot vertical cut to occur at Rehoboth Beach.  ||Peak wind gusts included 53 mph in Slaughter Beach (Sussex County), 45  mph in Lewes (Sussex County), 44 mph in Indian River (Sussex County) and Dover (Kent County), 38 mph at Rehoboth Beach and 33 mph at the New Castle County Airport. Precipitation totals included 3.69 inches in Newark (New Castle County), 3.33 inches in Glasgow (New Castle County), 3.32 inches at the New Castle County Airport, 2.07 inches in Georgetown (Sussex County), 2.04 inches in Rehoboth Beach (Sussex County), 1.77 inches in Milford (Sussex County), 1.53 inches in Smyrna (Kent County) and 1.25 inches in Dover (Kent County). The high tide at Reedys Point (New Castle County) peaked at 7.22 feet above mean lower low water at 524 p.m. EDT on the 6th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 7.2 feet above mean lower low water. ||Tropical Storm Hanna made landfall at about 320 a.m. EDT on September 6th near the border of North and South Carolina. The tropical storm proceeded to move northeast. At 8 a.m. EDT, Hanna was near Goldsboro, North Carolina; at 11 a.m. EDT just southeast of Emporia, Virginia; at 2 p.m. EDT near Williamsburg, Virginia, at 5 p.m. EDT in Dorchester County, Maryland. Hanna then cut across northwest Sussex County in Delaware and entered Delaware Bay and made landfall in New Jersey close to 7 p.m. EDT in eastern Cumberland County. At 8 p.m. EDT Hanna was located in northern Atlantic County, New Jersey and exited the state near Island Beach State Park in Ocean County. At 11 p.m. EDT, Hanna was about to make landfall in western Suffolk County in New York State. Hanna continued to move northeast through eastern Long Island and New England overnight on the 6th.

A secondary cold frontal passage coupled with an upper air disturbance aloft helped trigger some severe and flash flooding producing thunderstorms in Sussex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused roadway flooding in and around Bethany Beach. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around 3 inches in the area, most of which fell within one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary cold frontal passage coupled with an upper air disturbance aloft helped trigger some severe and flash flooding producing thunderstorms in Sussex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel size hail fell in Rehoboth Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary cold frontal passage coupled with an upper air disturbance aloft helped trigger some severe and flash flooding producing thunderstorms in Sussex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused the flooding of Delaware State Route 1 on the south end of the Indian River Inlet Bridge. Other stream and poor drainage flooding occurred in Indian River Hundred and Lewes and Rehoboth Hundred. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 6 inches across east central Sussex County with the highest estimates along the border between Indian River and Lewes and Rehoboth Hundreds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong for August upper air disturbance helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across Delaware. Severe thunderstorms started developing along the warm front in the morning and continued through the afternoon and into the evening until a cold front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped nickel size hail from Bear northeast through New Castle. The large hail shredded leaves off of trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter size hail fell in Wilmington Manor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several large oak and sycamore trees at the Brandywine Zoo in Wilmington. The downed trees damaged several exhibits. The In Flight exhibit was destroyed. A downed tree also punctured a hole in the roof af the animal hospital and damaged a storage shed. No animals were injured. The zoo remained closed until the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong for August upper air disturbance helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across Delaware. Severe thunderstorms started developing along the warm front in the morning and continued through the afternoon and into the evening until a cold front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding that entered a couple of basement apartments at the Dana Apartments on North Tatnall Street. About a dozen residents were evacuated from two basement apartments. Three apartments and families above the flooded apartments were also displaced because the electricity was turned off. The building sits lower than the street. Adjusted for hail Doppler Radar storm total estimates were between one inch and an inch and a quarter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably dry August occurred across the state of Delaware and created an agricultural drought for farms that did not have irrigation capabilities. The dry August was taking its toll on the corn crop. On a county weighted average August monthly precipitation ranged from 0.5 inches in Kent County to 0.8 inches in New Castle and Sussex Counties. Normal is around 4.8 inches. The statewide average monthly precipitation total of 0.99 inches was the driest August on record spanning back to 1895. The actual August monthly precipitation total at the New Castle County airport was 1.16 inches, 2.35 inches drier than normal and at the Georgetown Airport (Sussex County) 0.52 inches, 5.07 inches drier than normal. August 2008 was the 6th driest August on record at the New Castle County Airport.

The longest heat wave of the summer affected northern Delaware from July 16th through the 22nd and southern Delaware from July 16th through the 23rd. The heat wave ended in the northern part of the state on the 22nd as thunderstorms kept temperatures below 90 degrees and in the southern part of the state on the 23rh after a cold front moved through. High temperarures were in the lower to mid 90s. The combination of the temperatures and dew points produced apparant temperatures or heat indices of around 100F. Highest temperatures included 96 degrees at the New Castle County Airport and Bethany Beach (Sussex County), 95 degrees in Dover (Kent County) and 94 degrees in Georgetown (Sussex County).

Prior to the arrival of Tropical Storm Hanna, unseasonably dry weather continued in Delaware. Only two percent of the top soil in Delaware had adequate moisture. Eighty-five percent of the sampled soil conditions were described as very short on moisture. Stream flow was well below normal and Newark had to switch its water supply source from the White Clay Creek to its reservoir. The drop in freshwater runoff increased chloride and salt levels in tidal sections of the White Clay Creek and the Christina River. The United Water Company had to maintain an inflatable dam south of Stanton on the White Clay Creek to block salty tidal surges and trap freshwater supplies.||Starting with Tropical Storm Hanna, slightly above normal precipitation fell for the rest of the month. The rain came too late for the corn crop and early soybeans. Yields for corn were expected to be down as much as 50 percent on some farms. There was hope for the late soybean, wheat and barley crop. Farmers were hit with a double whammy as fuel and fertilizer costs rocketed. The state poultry industry was also feeling the pinch as food costs were rising because of the drought and shortages.  ||On a county weighted average September monthly precipitation ranged from 4.0 inches in Kent County to 4.7 inches in Sussex County and 5.0 inches in New Castle. Normal is around 3.7 inches.

An approaching cold front that ended a week long heat wave also triggered severe thunderstorms across Delaware during the afternoon and early evening on the 23rd. The thunderstorms caused outages to about 9,000 homes and businesses in New Castle County. There were still 6,000 homes and businesses without power at 11 p.m. EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a few trees in and around Delaware City. Fallen trees damaged two homes. Marble (half inch) size hail was also reported in Bear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front that ended a week long heat wave also triggered severe thunderstorms across Delaware during the afternoon and early evening on the 23rd. The thunderstorms caused outages to about 9,000 homes and businesses in New Castle County. There were still 6,000 homes and businesses without power at 11 p.m. EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a house fire in the Tenby Chase Development off of Baltimore Road in Hockessin. When firefighters arrived, they saw smoke billowing from the roof. Two people in the home escaped. It was under control within 20 minutes and the fire was contained to the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that persisted and intensified during the summer led to Delaware being declared a primary natural disaster area. The drought disaster declaration was requested as corn and soybean yields were 30 percent or more below average. Farmers have eight months to apply for loans to cover their losses. October 2008 was another dry month. On a county weighted average, monthly precipitation ranged from 1.3 inches in Sussex County, to 1.5 inches in Kent County to 2.0 inches in New Castle County. Normal is around 3.2 inches.  At the New Castle County Airport, the October monthly precipitation total of 1.81 inches was 1.27 inches drier than normal. At the Georgetown Airport (Sussex County), the October monthly precipitation total of 1.37 inches was 1.99 inches drier than normal.

Gusty northwest winds that reached as high as 63 mph occurred from the mid morning into the afternoon across Delaware.||Two homes were damaged by downed trees in New Castle (New Castle County). Peak wind gusts included 63 mph in Sandtown (Kent County), 48 mph in Milford (Sussex County), 47 mph in Dover (Kent County), Lewes (Sussex County) and the New Castle County Airport and 45 mph in New Castle (New Castle County). Most of the peak wind gusts occurred during the early afternoon on the 28th as the pressure gradient (difference) tightened quickly between the departing and intensifying low pressure system moving into New England and a high pressure system moving east from the central United States.||A low pressure system developed on the stalled front over North Carolina on the afternoon of the 27th. It slowly moved northeast and intensified rapidly. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 28th it was just off the New Jersey Coast and had a central pressure of 1003 millibars. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 28th, the low pressure system was approaching central Long Island, New York and the pressure lowered to 995 millibars. It proceeded to move northeast and was a 989 millibar low pressure system near Hartford, Connecticut at 2 p.m. EDT on the 28th and a 986 millibar low pressure system along the northern Vermont and New Hampshire border at 8 p.m. EDT on the 28th. By evening this low pressure system was far enough away that winds decreased.

The drought that persisted and intensified during the summer led to Delaware being declared a primary natural disaster area. The drought disaster declaration was requested as corn and soybean yields were 30 percent or more below average. Farmers have eight months to apply for loans to cover their losses. A strong cold front helped develop a squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through Delaware during the afternoon of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were knocked down on the south side of Georgetown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front helped develop a squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through Delaware during the afternoon of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused two-thirds of Dover to lose power for about 90 minutes during the afternoon of the 9th.
